era_context: The early 12th century is marked by the consolidation of the Crusader States in the Levant (established 1096–1099), the rise of the Seljuk Sultanate of Rum in Anatolia, the Norman Kingdom of Sicily's expansion in the Mediterranean, and the Northern Song Dynasty's cultural and economic flourishing in China despite military pressure from the Jin Dynasty. In Europe, feudalism and the Church remain dominant; the Investiture Controversy has recently been resolved (1122).
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and the Steppe (sources primarily from Chinese, Islamic, and Byzantine perspectives)
The Americas (no written sources; archaeological evidence not systematically integrated)

events.title: Emperor Huizong abdicates; Qinzong becomes ruler of Northern Song
events.description: Emperor Huizong of the Northern Song Dynasty abdicates in 1126, transferring power to his son Qinzong (r. 1125–1127). Huizong's abdication is partly motivated by his desire to avoid direct responsibility for the deteriorating military situation as the Jin Dynasty advances southward. Qinzong's brief reign (1125–1127) is marked by military crisis and the imminent collapse of the Northern Song, which will fall to the Jin conquest in 1127. Huizong's abdication represents a significant moment in the transition from the Northern to the Southern Song.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)
Song Dynasty official histories and records

events.certainty_note: Huizong's abdication and Qinzong's accession are well-documented in Chinese historical records. However, some sources date Huizong's abdication to 1125, creating minor dating uncertainty.

events.title: Jin Dynasty advances toward Kaifeng; Northern Song military crisis
events.description: The Jin Dynasty, led by the Jurchen people, continues its military advance toward Kaifeng, the capital of the Northern Song Dynasty. By 1126, the Jin have conquered much of northern China and are preparing for the final assault on Kaifeng, which will occur in 1127. The Song Dynasty faces a severe military crisis, with the Jin advancing rapidly and the Song military unable to mount an effective defense. This period marks the beginning of the end for the Northern Song and the prelude to the 'Jingkang Incident' (the capture of the Song emperors) in 1127.

sources.name: Song Dynasty official histories and records

events.certainty_note: The Jin advance toward Kaifeng in 1126 is well-documented in Chinese historical records as a prelude to the 1127 conquest.

events.title: Baldwin II rules Kingdom of Jerusalem; Crusader consolidation
events.region: Kingdom of Jerusalem and Crusader States
events.description: Baldwin II (r. 1118–1131) continues to rule the Kingdom of Jerusalem, consolidating Crusader control over the Levant. By 1126, the Crusader States have been established for approximately 27 years (since 1099) and are in a period of relative stability and consolidation. Baldwin II is engaged in military campaigns against surrounding Islamic powers and is also involved in the development of military and administrative structures to support the Crusader presence. The period is marked by the growth of military orders, including the Knights Templar (formally recognized in 1129).

sources.name: William of Tyre, History of the Crusades
Fulcher of Chartres, History of the Expedition to Jerusalem

events.certainty_note: Baldwin II's rule and the Crusader States' consolidation are well-documented in both Crusader and Islamic sources.

events.title: Seljuk Sultanate of Rum consolidates power under Masud I
events.description: Masud I (r. 1116–1156) continues to consolidate the power of the Seljuk Sultanate of Rum in Anatolia. By 1126, Masud I has established himself as a major regional power, competing with the Byzantine Empire and the Crusader States for control of Anatolia and the eastern Mediterranean. The Seljuk Sultanate of Rum represents a major Islamic power in the region and a significant challenge to both Byzantine and Crusader interests.

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History (al-Kamil fi al-Ta'rikh)
Byzantine sources (Anna Komnena, Alexiad, and later chronicles)
Historiographic consensus on Seljuk Sultanate of Rum

events.certainty_note: Masud I's consolidation of power is well-attested in Islamic and Byzantine sources, though specific events within 1126 are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Norman expansion in Sicily and southern Italy continues
events.description: Roger II, Count of Sicily (r. 1105–1154), continues to consolidate Norman power in Sicily and southern Italy. By 1126, Roger II is in the process of unifying the Norman territories and establishing the Kingdom of Sicily, which will be formally proclaimed in 1130. The Norman expansion represents a significant shift in Mediterranean politics and the rise of a new major power that will challenge both papal and imperial authority.
key_figures: Roger II (Count of Sicily, later King, r. 1105–1154)
events.certainty_note: Roger II's consolidation of power is well-attested in Norman and papal sources, though specific events within 1126 are not always precisely dated.
cross_references: Norman expansion in southern Italy (11th–12th centuries)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Huizong's abdication is sometimes placed in 1125 rather than 1126 in some sources, creating minor historiographic uncertainty. The 'Jingkang Incident' (capture of the Song emperors) is sometimes dated to late 1126 in some sources, though 1127 is the more common dating. No significant disconfirming evidence has been identified for the other events listed.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1126 is moderate but unevenly distributed. Chinese sources provide detailed accounts of the Song Dynasty's military crisis and the Jin advance. Western European sources document the Crusader States and Norman expansion. However, the surviving sources are often compiled decades or centuries after events, and the record is heavily weighted toward elite, literate cultures. The experiences of common people, particularly in regions experiencing military conflict, are largely absent from the surviving record. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Central Asia are virtually absent from the surviving written record for this year.

graph_edges.note: Huizong's abdication and Qinzong's accession lead to the military crisis that culminates in the Jin conquest of Kaifeng in 1127
Jin advance toward Kaifeng in 1126 leads to the conquest of Kaifeng in 1127
Baldwin II's consolidation of Crusader control leads to the formal recognition of the Knights Templar in 1129
Masud I's consolidation of Seljuk power continues until the Byzantine victory at the Battle of Myriokephalon in 1176
Roger II's consolidation of Norman power leads to the formal establishment of the Kingdom of Sicily in 1130
